title: Short-Term Trading with the 50-Day Line
subtitle: Faster entries and exits within a Stage 2 uptrend

lessons.title: The Setup — Daily Chart with 50-Day SMA
lessons.content:
This module gathers every short-term timing rule in one place. Short-term trading is optional — it layers a daily-chart timing tool on top of the weekly-chart stage method you already know.

The Chart: Short-term traders add a daily chart with a 50-day SMA (which is the daily equivalent of the 10-week line) to find faster entries and exits.

The Non-Negotiable Context: The stock must still be in a macro Stage 2 uptrend on the weekly chart. The daily chart only times entries within an established weekly uptrend — it never overrides it.

This is the critical rule that most short-term traders forget: if the weekly chart says Stage 4, no daily chart signal gives you permission to buy. The weekly chart is always the master.

Why use the 50-day line for short-term timing?
→ It is the daily equivalent of the 30-week SMA
→ In a Stage 2 uptrend, daily pullbacks to the 50-day line are low-risk entries
→ It gives you a tighter, more precise entry than waiting for weekly signals
→ It also gives you an earlier exit signal in Stage 3

→ Daily chart + 50-day SMA = short-term timing tool
→ Weekly chart + 30-week SMA = master trend direction
→ Daily never overrides weekly
→ Only use this module inside confirmed weekly Stage 2

lessons.title: Short-Term Entries
lessons.visual_caption: Daily chart with 50-day SMA. Arrows mark the entry points where price dips to touch the rising 50-day line and bounces. These are the short-term buying opportunities.
lessons.content:
The short-term entry is simple and precise. There is only one setup.

The Entry: On the daily chart, wait for price to dip and touch the rising 50-day SMA, then buy as it bounces off the line on low volume.

The ideal setup:
→ Weekly chart confirms Stage 2 uptrend
→ Daily chart shows price pulling back toward the 50-day SMA
→ Volume on the pullback is declining (healthy rest, not a reversal)
→ Price finds support at the 50-day line and bounces
→ Volume picks up on the bounce day

The Restriction: Never take a short-term entry when the daily price is below a flat or falling 50-day line. If the 50-day line is flat or declining, the daily trend has changed. Even if the weekly is still Stage 2, the short-term timing is unfavourable.

Patience applies here too:
Do not force an entry when price is extended far above the 50-day line. Wait for the pullback to the line. It will come in every healthy Stage 2 stock.

→ Wait for price to touch the rising 50-day SMA
→ Volume must be declining on the pullback (healthy)
→ Buy the bounce off the line
→ Never buy below a flat or falling 50-day SMA

lessons.title: Short-Term Exits
lessons.content:
The short-term exit is faster and more decisive than the long-term exit. This is how short-term traders protect quick gains.

The Core Rule: Sell 100% of the short-term position the moment price crosses down through a flattening or declining 50-day line. Do not wait. Do not hope for recovery.

In Stage 3: When a topping stock loses its 50-day line as that line flattens, the short-term trader is fully out — well before the long-term investor's 30-week exit signal. This is the major advantage of short-term timing: you exit earlier, with more profit protected.

In Stage 4: Short-term traders never wait for the weekly 30-week line to break. The 50-day cross-down is the exit. No new long entry is taken under a falling 50-day line.

Long-Term vs Short-Term Exit Comparison:

Long-term investor:
→ Sell half when 30-week SMA flattens (Stage 3)
→ Sell rest when price closes below 30-week SMA (Stage 4 entry)

Short-term trader:
→ Sell 100% when price crosses below a flattening 50-day SMA
→ This is earlier and protects more profit

The tradeoff:
Shorter signals mean more trades and more decision-making. The long-term method requires less frequent action. Choose based on your temperament and time availability.

→ Cross below flattening 50-day SMA = sell 100% immediately
→ Shorter signal = earlier exit, more profit protected
→ More frequent signals = more active management required
→ Choose the timeframe that matches your temperament

lessons.title: Long-Term vs Short-Term — Side by Side
lessons.content:
Here is the complete comparison between the two approaches. Neither is superior — they serve different types of investors.

Reference Line:
→ Long-term investor: 30-week SMA on weekly chart
→ Short-term trader: 50-day SMA on daily chart

Entry:
→ Long-term: Breakout above resistance, or pullback kiss to 30-week line
→ Short-term: Bounce off the rising 50-day line on daily chart

Exit:
→ Long-term: Sell half in Stage 3; sell rest when price closes below 30-week line
→ Short-term: Full exit on a cross below the 50-day line

Frequency of trades:
→ Long-term: Fewer trades, longer holding periods, less monitoring required
→ Short-term: More trades, shorter holding periods, daily monitoring required

Best for:
→ Long-term: Investors who check charts weekly and want to capture full trends
→ Short-term: Active traders who monitor daily and want tighter risk management

Important: Both methods require the weekly Stage 2 confirmation. The short-term method is just a more precise timing layer — it never replaces the weekly stage analysis.

For most investors learning this method, the long-term approach is recommended first. Master the weekly chart and the four stages. Add daily timing only after you are comfortable reading stages confidently.

→ Both methods require weekly Stage 2 confirmation
→ Short-term = more precise timing, more active
→ Long-term = less frequent, captures full trend
→ Start with long-term, add daily timing later

questions.question: Which line do short-term traders use on the daily chart to time pullback entries?
questions.explanation: Within an active Stage 2 uptrend, short-term traders buy bounces off the rising 50-day SMA on the daily chart. This is the daily equivalent of the 30-week SMA used for weekly analysis.

questions.question: What context must always be true before taking a short-term 50-day entry?
options: The weekly chart must be in a Stage 2 uptrend
The stock must be in Stage 4
Volume must be at a record low
Earnings must be released that same week
questions.explanation: The daily 50-day tool only times entries inside an established weekly Stage 2 uptrend. It never overrides the weekly trend. The weekly chart is always the master.

questions.question: What should a short-term trader do when price drops below a flattening or declining 50-day SMA?
options: Buy the dip because it looks cheap
Hold until the weekly 30-week line breaks
questions.explanation: The 50-day cross-down is the short-term protective exit. The trader sells fully to preserve capital — ahead of the long-term signal. This is the main advantage of short-term timing: earlier exit with more profit protected.
